content.problemStatement:
You are given a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. Find the number of ways to split it into one or more non-empty contiguous subsequences B1,B2,…,BkB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_kB1​,B2​,…,Bk​ such that the following condition is satisfied: for each iii (1≤i≤k1 \le i \le k1≤i≤k), the XOR of all elements in BiB_iBi​ is divisible by 2i−12^{i-1}2i−1.

Since this number can be enormous, compute it modulo 1,000,000,0071,000,000,0071,000,000,007 (109+710^9 + 7109+7).


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the number of ways to split AAA modulo 1,000,000,0071,000,000,0071,000,000,007 (109+710^9 + 7109+7).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 100000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the sequence. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of valid splittings modulo 1 000 000 007.

solution.approach:
Let P[i] be the prefix xor (P[0]=0, P[i]=A1 xor … xor Ai). The xor of a segment (j+1 … i) equals P[i] xor P[j]. For the i‑th block we need (P[i] xor P[j]) to be divisible by 2^{i‑1}, i.e. its lowest (i‑1) bits must be zero. This is equivalent to P[i] and P[j] being equal modulo 2^{i‑1}.

We process the array from left to right and keep DP[i][k] = number of ways to split the prefix ending at position i into exactly k blocks satisfying the condition. The transition is
    DP[i][k] = Σ DP[j][k‑1] over all j < i with (P[i] xor P[j]) & ((1<<(k‑1))-1) == 0.

For a fixed k the condition depends only on P[i] modulo M = 2^{k‑1}. Therefore we maintain, for each possible block count k, a hash‑map that stores for every remainder r the sum of DP values of prefixes that end with k‑1 blocks and have P prefix remainder r. When we are at position i we can obtain DP[i][k] by a single hash‑map lookup using key = P[i] & (M‑1). After DP[i][k] is known we insert it into the map for k+1 (which uses modulus 2^{k}).

The divisor grows exponentially; after about 60 blocks it exceeds the maximum possible xor (≤ 2^54), so we only need to consider k up to 61. The algorithm therefore runs in O(N·K) with K≈61, easily fast for N=10^5.

Implementation details:
1. Compute prefix xor array.
2. Initialise a map for k=1 (modulus 1) with a single entry remainder 0 → 1 (the empty prefix with 0 blocks).
3. For each position i (1…N) compute DP[i][k] for all k by looking up the current map for k.
4. After all k are processed, update the map for k+1 with the newly obtained DP values.
5. The answer for a test case is Σ DP[N][k] for k≥1. All operations are performed modulo 1 000 000 007.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N · K) where K = 61 (constant). For the whole input O( Σ N ).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N · K) in the worst case for the hash‑maps, but each map stores at most N entries, so ≤ about 6·10^6 key‑value pairs (well within limits).
